title: Human Dignity as Non-Negotiable
core_moral_threshold: A system is moral only when every person is treated as possessing inherent dignity; no one may be reduced to a tool, scapegoat, or expendable cost.
trigger_patterns: people are treated as replaceable units
harm is justified for 'the greater good'
minorities are blamed to preserve order
rights depend on loyalty to power
warning_signs: public shaming in meetings or comms
reflection_questions: Who is being diminished or humiliated by this system?
Where are people treated as means rather than ends?
What would change if dignity were the first constraint?
Who becomes a scapegoat when pressure rises?
What repair is owed when dignity is breached?
safe_actions: ban humiliating practices and model respectful discourse
document dignity-impact for high-stakes decisions
create protection pathways for vulnerable stakeholders
require repair actions when dignity harm occurs
treat dignity as a measurable governance requirement

title: Responsibility Over Substitution
core_moral_threshold: Ethical repair requires personal and collective responsibility; guilt cannot be transferred to an innocent party to preserve the system.
trigger_patterns: blame is shifted to protect leadership
suffering is framed as redemptive necessity
people are punished for systemic failures
confession is demanded without structural change
warning_signs: people hide problems to avoid blame
reflection_questions: Who is being asked to carry responsibility that is not theirs?
What systemic cause is being disguised as an individual fault?
What would genuine repair look like here?
Where does accountability end and scapegoating begin?
What commitments prevent repetition?
safe_actions: separate accountability from blame in incident reviews
require leaders to own upstream causes and corrections
establish repair commitments with timelines and owners
protect those who surface problems early
track recurrence and learning, not just punishment

title: Law as Protection, Not Domination
core_moral_threshold: Rules are moral only when they protect the vulnerable and constrain power; law becomes corrupt when used to dominate or exempt the powerful.
trigger_patterns: compliance is used as a shield for harm
warning_signs: rapid proliferation of rules after criticism
legalistic arguments used to dismiss harm
reflection_questions: Whom does this rule protect, and whom does it burden?
Where are rules being used to silence rather than safeguard?
Which exceptions reveal power capture?
What would fair enforcement require?
Where must responsibility override procedure?
safe_actions: audit rule enforcement for consistency and bias
publish clear exception criteria and approvals
add protected channels for principled dissent
require harm review alongside compliance checks
simplify rules that primarily serve control

title: Argument as a Truth Method
core_moral_threshold: Truth is strengthened through structured disagreement; systems decay when debate is punished and unanimity is manufactured.
trigger_patterns: information is filtered before reaching decision-makers
warning_signs: silence in meetings despite obvious risks
only one narrative is allowed publicly
people speak privately but not formally
reflection_questions: Where is disagreement being suppressed?
Which arguments are missing from the table?
Who cannot speak without risk?
What would a safe debate structure look like?
How can dissent be protected without forcing agreement?
safe_actions: establish red-team roles and dissent protocols
separate critique of ideas from critique of persons
create psychological safety in key decision forums
require at least one documented counter-argument in major decisions
reward truth-telling behaviors

title: Justice and Fair Measure
core_moral_threshold: A system is moral only when it uses fair measures and consistent standards; distortion of measures is a core corruption mechanism.
trigger_patterns: metrics are gamed to appear successful
reporting is shaped to please power
warning_signs: widening gap between metrics and lived reality
reflection_questions: What measures are distorted, and who benefits?
Where do incentives reward deception?
What reality is being hidden by the dashboard?
What would a fair measure look like here?
What would we report if fear disappeared?
safe_actions: audit metrics for gaming and perverse incentives
add ground-truth sampling and independent verification
protect messengers of bad news
publish definitions of metrics and changes over time
tie rewards to integrity signals, not only outcomes

title: Truth-Telling Without Retaliation
core_moral_threshold: Truth cannot flourish where retaliation exists; moral systems structurally protect those who speak in good faith.
warning_signs: unusually optimistic reporting despite evident problems
reflection_questions: What truths are missing because people are afraid?
Who has been punished for honesty?
What protections are absent?
How can truth be surfaced without coercion?
What would restore trust in reporting?
safe_actions: implement anti-retaliation safeguards with independent oversight
create confidential reporting and mediation channels
separate evaluation of people from evaluation of truth claims
require leaders to respond with corrective action, not defensiveness
track psychological safety as a leading indicator

title: Boundaries on Power
core_moral_threshold: Power must be constrained by explicit limits; a system becomes immoral when any person or group becomes the final authority.
trigger_patterns: institutions are captured by personal loyalty
reflection_questions: Where has authority become personalized?
Which limits on power are missing or unenforced?
Who cannot question decisions safely?
What structure must stand above every individual?
How can accountability be made non-optional?
safe_actions: anchor authority in written principles and independent oversight
separate person from role in decision records
publish accountability mechanisms and escalation paths
establish protected dissent and review processes
limit exception power with transparent approvals

title: Repair and Return
core_moral_threshold: Moral life depends on repair after harm; systems that cannot admit wrongs cannot evolve toward justice.
trigger_patterns: errors are denied to protect image
warning_signs: lack of restitution or remediation pathways
reflection_questions: What harm requires repair here?
What would meaningful restitution look like?
What prevents admitting error—fear, pride, incentives?
How can repair be made structural, not personal?
What would 'return' look like in practice?
safe_actions: create clear remediation and restitution mechanisms
publish post-incident learning reports and actions
separate accountability from humiliation
resource repair work as core infrastructure
measure trust recovery over time

title: Non-Coercion and Voluntary Covenant
core_moral_threshold: Durable moral alignment cannot be forced; systems must rely on voluntary commitment, consent, and principled limits rather than compulsion.
warning_signs: moral language used to justify force
reflection_questions: Where are we using pressure instead of consent?
What would voluntary commitment look like here?
Which protections enable authentic participation?
How do we reduce coercion while preserving safety?
What is the smallest change that increases trust?
safe_actions: replace loyalty tests with principle-based governance
build opt-in pilots where feasible
protect dissent through independent safeguards
increase transparency and explainability of rules
treat trust as a measurable system requirement
